name: HMS P 32 (P 32)
events: The history of HMS P 32 as compiled on this page is extracted from P 32's patrol reports and logbooks and was compiled in December 2013. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in February 2026.
2 May 1941 P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) departed her builders yard for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR).
[No log of HMS P 32 is available for the month of May 1941 so details for this month will be missing.] (1)

3 May 1941 P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training. She had just completed her acceptance trials and was taken over from the shipyards and placed in full commission upon arrival at Holy Loch. (1)
26 May 1941 For the daily positions of HMS P 32 during her passage from Holy Loch to Gibraltar see the map below;

26 May 1941 At 0700 B/26, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) departed Holy Loch for Gibraltar. She was escorted south through the Irish Sea by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) until 0300 B/28 when they parted company near Bishop Rock.

HMS P 32 was routed as follows; Position 'A' (45°00'N, 09°40'W) at 0500 B/31. Position 'B' (43°00'N, 10°20'W) at 1900 B/31. Position 'C' (39°00'N, 10°40'W) at 2000 A/1. Position 'D' (36°40'N, 09°30'W) at 1200 A/2. Position 'E' (350° - Cape Spartel - 10 miles) at 0700 A/3. (2)
31 May 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) is depth charged and damaged by a German Heinkel 59 seaplane in the Bay of Biscay. Moderate damage was caused to P 32.

Later the same day P 32 sighted a westbound tanker but she did not attack her as to do not give her position away in her damaged state and it was considered very well possible that this tanker was Spanish.

This tanker however, we believe, was the German naval tanker Gedania (8966 GRT, built 1920) en-route into the Atlantic on a supply mission for U-boats. Gedania was captured on 4 June 1941 by the Ocean Boarding Vessel HMS Marsdale (Lt.Cdr. D.H.F. Armstrong, RNR). According to the KTB (Kriegstagebuch = logbook) of the German Marinegruppenbefehlshabers West a German reconnaissance aircraft had spotted her proceeding westwards in grid BF 7564 which is centred in position 44°15'N, 09°26'W.

0400 B/31 - In position 46°10'N, 09°06'W, sighted two small vessels burning lights. P 32 closed to investigate and the vessels were identified as trawlers proceeding on course 120° at 11 knots. It was thought this may have been rescue vessels which had been searching for survivors of the Bismarck. [We consider this to be unlikely and this were most likely just fishing vessels.]

1145 B/31 - P 32 was in position 'A' of her route which was 45°00'N, 09°40'W. She arrived late in this position but no signal was sent as it was feared the Germans would fix the position of the submarine through D/F.
1600 B/31 - In position 44°24'N, 09°46'W, sighted a Heinkel 59 bearing 110°. She was seen banking towards. Dived.
1601 B/31 - When at 70 feet and still on our original course three depth charges exploded causing damage to the after hydroplanes and the battery. Went to 150 feet.
1640 B/31 - Heard three detonations.
2035 B/31 - Surfaced in position 44°15'N, 09°54'W and resumed passage to Gibraltar.
2148 B/31 - In position 44°03'N, 09°58'W sighted a tanker hull down.
2150 B/31 - Dived to start an attack. Course of the tanker was 295° at 11 knots.

The attack was not carried though as by plot the tanker appeared to have sailed from Ferrol, Spain. The tanker also remained on a steady course. The tanker appeared to be unarmed. The tanker could not be positively identified as enemy as it was between the light horizon and the submarine.
The tanker was not seen to fly an ensign and no markings were visible. The tanker was painted grey and had yellow upperworks.
2255 B/31 - Surfaced and resumed passage to Gibraltar. (3)

31 May 1941 In a signal timed 1110 B/31, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) of the following; Two British trawlers on patrol off Lisbon. Two Portuguese ships are exercising off Gezimbra near position 37°00'N, 08°40'W. It is believed four U-boats are operating between Latitudes 35°N and 37°N and meridian 07°W and 10°W.
In a signal timed 1154 A/31, F.O.C.N.A. informed HMS P 32 of the following; Enemy surface unit indicated by D/F bearing at 2042 Z/30 may have been within 200 miles of position 45°30'N, 14°00'W. (4)

1 Jun 1941 At 0245 B/1, in position 43°31'N, 10°20'W, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted a merchant vessel which was burning her navigation lights. It was seen to be a fully illuminated 2000 tons merchant vessel proceeding on course 060° at 10 knots. This was most likely a Spanish vessel en-route to a port in northern Spain.

At 0600 B/1, HMS P 32 arrived in position 'B' which was 43°00'N, 10°20'W. She was still behind of the schedule but no signal was sent for the same reason as the day before (fear of being fixed by D/F). (2)

2 Jun 1941 At 0318 B/2, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1630 A/1 was received which stated the following; Several A/S operations are in progress in the approach to the Straits of Gibraltar. Adhere strictly to your route and programme. Inform me if unable to reach position 'E' within two hours of 0700 A/3. Onward escort will not he provided.

At 0401 B/2, HMS P 32 transmitted a signal, timed 2045 B/31 but this should have been 2045 B/1. This signal reported that P 32 would be late at position 'C' and that she had been bombed and required docking on arrival at Gibraltar.
At 0709 B/2, HMS P 32 was in position 'C' which was 39°00'N, 10°40'W.

At 1336 B/2, the signal timed 0710 A/2 from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) was received. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but most likely P 32 was ordered to give details on the timing she would be positions 'D' and 'E' of her route and at her ETA at Gibraltar.]
At 1623 B/2, HMS P 32 transmitted her signal timed 1330 B/2 reporting her ETA at Gibraltar and at positions 'D' and 'E'.

At 1843 B/2, in position 37°30'N, 09°50'W, a merchant vessel was seen bearing 135° at a range of 12 miles. It was subsequently seen to be a Portugese merchant vessel of about 800 tons proceeding on course 197° at 13 knots.

At 2159 A/2, [The clock had been put back on board HMS P 32 now] F.O.C.N.A.'s signal timed 1710 A/2 was received. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but most likely it contained instructions to make rendezvous with an escort.]
At 2310 A/2, HMS P 32 was in position 'D' (36°40'N, 09°30'W). (2)

3 Jun 1941 At 0630 A/3, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) arrived in position 36°23'N, 08°20'W in which rendezvous was to be made with the trawler (armed boarding vessel) HMS Scottish (Cdr.(Retd.) S.A. Brooks, OBE, RN) but the trawler was not there.

At 0810 A/3, HMS P 32 transmitted her signal timed 0705 A/3, which reported that HMS Scottish was not at the rendezvous and that HMS P 32 was proceeding to position 'E' keeping clear of the area in which U-boat activity had been reported.
At 0845 A/3, a fix was taken and it was discovered that HMS P 32 was 14 miles further to the east as expected.
At 1515 A/3, in position 36°04'N, 06°31'W, a merchant vessel was sighted hull down on bearing 050° at a range of 14 miles. Course of this merchant vessel was 300°.
At 1645 A/3, rendezvous was made with HMS Scottish and course was set in company to proceed to Gibraltar.
At 1725 A/3, HMS P 32 and HMS Scottish arrived in position 'E' (35°57'N, 05°58'W).
At 2230 A/3, HMS P 32 and HMS Scottish arrived at Gibraltar. (2)
10 Jun 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) is docked in No.3 graving dock at the Gibraltar Dockyard for repairs to the damage sustained on 31 May. (5)
18 Jun 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) is undocked. (5)
21 Jun 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) is docked again at the Gibraltar Dockyard but this time in No.2 graving dock. (5)
23 Jun 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) is undocked. (5)
3 Jul 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) completed her repairs at Gibraltar. (6)
5 Jul 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) conducted trials and exercises off Gibraltar. (6)
6 Jul 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (6)
9 Jul 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (6)
14 Jul 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) conducted exercises off Gibraltar. (6)

17 Jul 1941 At 1815 A/17, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off Cagliari, Sardinia, Italy during operation Substance. She was to proceed to Malta upon completion of this patrol.
For the daily positions of HMS P 32 during this passage see the map below.
18 Jul 1941 At 0030 A/18, in position 36°09'N, 04°30'W, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) passed a fully illuminated merchant vessel proceeding on course 280° at 10 knots.

At 2026 A/18, in position 36°47'N, 00°30'W, HMS P 32 sighted a Vichy-French merchant vessel bearing 060° at a range of 12 miles. Course and speed of this merchant vessel were 160° at 12 knots. Most likely this vessel was en-route to Oran. (7)

19 Jul 1941 At 1724 A/19, in position 38°02'N, 03°14'E, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted two Vichy-French merchant vessels bearing 330° at a range of 12 miles. Course of these vessels was 185° at a speed of 10 knots. Most likely these two merchant vessels were en-route to Algiers. (7)

21 Jul 1941 At 1020 A/21, south of Sardinia, in position 38°35'N, 08°53'E, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted a two-masted schooner, hull down on bearing 105° at a range of 7 miles. This schooner appeared to lay stopped in this position all day. Most likely this was an Italian A/S schooner equipped with a listening device.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

1228 A/21, in position 38°40'N, 08°56'E, HMS P 32 sighted a 4000-ton merchant vessel bearing 320° at a range of 6 miles. Course of this merchant vessel was 070°. P 32 closed to attack but the enemy vessel altered course away to 025°. At the same time another, small, merchant vessel was seen steering on course 200° but this vessel also altered course away but to the north-westward.

This were possibly the Italian transport Fedora (5016 GRT, built 1910) carrying phosphate which sailed from Bone (Algeria) at 1900 B/20 for Cagliari. The smaller ship may have been the Italian transport Etruria (2633 GRT, built 1906) had sailed from Naples at 2000 B/19, at 9 knots, for Bone (to pick up a cargo of phosphate). It is possible that she received a signal to turn back (which explains her movements) when it was learned that Force H was at sea and proceeding eastward.
At 1300 A/21, HMS P 32 entered her patrol area. Course was set to patrol off Cavoli Island.
At 1940 A/21, off Cagliari Bay, in position 38°44'N, 09°08'E, a two-masted schooner was sighted bearing 180° at a range of 6 miles. Course of this schooner was 330°.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (7)
Passage through the Straits of Gibraltar of the eastbound convoy and sailing from Gibraltar of the remaining ships involved in the operation.

Around 0130 B/21 convoy WS 9C passed the Straits of Gibraltar. The convoy at that moment consisted of six merchant ships; City of Pretoria (8049 GRT, built 1937), Deucalion (7516 GRT, built 1930), Durham (10893 GRT, built 1934), Melbourne Star (11076 GRT, built 1936), Port Chalmers (8535 GRT, built 1933) and Sydney Star (11095 GRT, built 1936).

At the time they passed through the Straits they were escorted by HMS Nelson (Capt. T.H. Troubridge, RN), HMS Edinburgh (Capt. H.W. Faulkner, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral E.N. Syfret, RN), HMS Manxman (Capt. R.K. Dickson, RN), HMS Lightning (Cdr. R.G. Stewart, RN), HMAS Nestor (Cdr. A.S. Rosenthal, RAN), HMS Avon Vale (Lt.Cdr. P.A.R. Withers, RN), HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, RN) and HMS Farndale (Cdr. S.H. Carlill, RN).

HMS Manchester (Capt. H. Drew, DSC, RN), HMS Arethusa (Capt. A.C. Chapman, RN), HMS Cossack (Capt. E.L. Berthon, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr. R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, RN) departed Gibraltar around 0200 B/21 escorting troopship Leinster (4302 GRT, built 1937) which was to join the convoy. However Leinster grounded while leaving Gibraltar and had to left behind. The small fleet tanker RFA Brown Ranger (3417 GRT, built 1941, master D.B.C. Ralph) left Gibraltar around the same time escorted by the destroyer HMS Beverley (Lt.Cdr. J. Grant, RN).

About one hour later, around 0300 B/21, HMS Renown (Rear-Admiral R.R. McGrigor, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral J.F. Somerville, KCB, DSO, RN), HMS Ark Royal (Capt. L.E.H. Maund, RN), HMS Hermione (Capt. G.N. Oliver, RN), HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.F. de Salis, RN), HMS Fearless (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN), HMS Firedrake (Lt.Cdr. S.H. Norris, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Foresight (Cdr. J.S.C. Salter, RN), HMS Forester (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Tancock, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Foxhound (Cdr. G.H. Peters, DSC, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.C. Robinson, RN) and HMS Duncan (Lt.Cdr. A.N. Rowell, RN) departed Gibraltar to give convoy for the convoy during the passage to Malta.
At sea the forces were redistributed; Force H, the cover force HMS Renown, HMS Nelson, HMS Ark Royal, HMS Hermione, HMS Faulknor, HMS Foresight, HMS Forester, HMS Fury, HMS Lightning and HMS Duncan.

Force X, the close escort for the convoy HMS Edinburgh, HMS Manchester, HMS Arethusa, HMS Manxman, HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Fearless, HMS Firedrake, HMS Foxhound, HMS Avon Vale, HMS Eridge and HMS Farndale.
Plan for the operation

Force H was to cover the convoy until it reached the narrows between Sicily and Tunisia. Force X was to escort the convoy all the way to Malta. Ships of Force X also had troops for Malta on board that had been taken to Gibraltar by troopship Pasteur. On 23 July 1941, the day the eastbound convoy would reach ‘the narrows’ five empty transports and two tankers would depart Malta for Gibraltar (Convoy MG 1) The seven empty transports were; Group 1 (speed 17 knots) HMS Breconshire (9776 GRT, built 1939), Talabot (6798 GRT, built 1936),
Group 2 (speed 14 knots) Thermopylae (6655 GRT, built 1930), Amerika (10218 GRT, built 1930),

Group 3 (speed 12 knots) Settler (6202 GRT, built 1939), Tanker Svenor (7616 GRT, built 1931) and Tanker Hoegh Hood (9351 GRT, built 1936) These were escorted by the destroyer HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN) which had been repairing and refitting at Malta.

Through intelligence it was known that the Italian Navy had five battleships operational (three of them at Taranto) and about ten cruisers divided between Taranto, Palermo and Messina. The Italian Air Force had about 50 torpedo planes and 150 bombers (30 of which were dive bombers) stationed in Sardinia and Sicily, roughly half of each type on both islands.

The Royal Air Force was able to be of more help than during the previous convoy trip from Gibraltar to Malta last January. Aircraft from Gibraltar conducted A/S patrols for the fleet during the first two days of the passage to the east. Also patrols were flown between Sardinia and the coast of Africa, while aircraft from Malta conducted reconnaissance between Sardinia and Sicily, besides watching the Italian ports. Malta would also provide fighter escort for Force X and the convoy after Force H would part with them and HMS Ark Royal could no longer provide fighter cover for them.

During the operation eight submarines (HMS Olympus (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Dymott, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN), HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HrMs O 21 (Lt.Cdr. J.F. van Dulm, RNN)) were on patrol to report and attack Italian warships that might be sailed to intercept the convoy.
The passage East, 22 July 1941

On 22 July the destroyers from Force X oiled from the Brown Ranger two at a time. A task that took about 10 hours. Having completed the oiling of the destroyers the Brown Ranger and her escort returned to Gibraltar. An Italian aircraft had reported Force H in the morning but the convoy and Force X, at that moment about 100 nautical miles to the south-westward, appeared not to have been sighed. At 2317 B/22 the Italian submarine Diaspro missed HMS Renown with torpedoes. HMAS Nestor sighted the torpedo tracks and was able to warn HMS Renown which was then able to avoid the torpedoes by doing an emergency turn to port.
The passage East and attacks by the Italian Air Force, 23 July 1941

Force H rejoined the convoy around 0800 B/23 as the British were now approaching the danger area. Shadowing aircraft had already reported the position of the fleet that morning and heavy air attacks soon followed.

The first came at 0945 hours, a well times combination of nine high level bombers and six or seven torpedo planes approaching from the north-east. HMS Ark Royal had eleven fighters up, which met the bombers about 20 miles from the fleet. They managed to down two of the nine bombers but unfortunately three Fulmars were shot down by the enemy. The other seven bombers came on working round the head of the screen of destroyers to attack the convoy from the starboard beam at a height of 10000 feet. Their bombs fell harmlessly amongst the leading ships as they altered course to avoid the attack. The torpedo planes however were more successful. They came from ahead out of the sun, flying low, and as the destroyers opened fire they divided into groups of two or three and to attack the convoy on both sides. Two aircraft attacked HMS Fearless, stationed ahead in the screen, dropping their torpedoes at ranges of 1500 and 800 yards from a height of 70 feet. The destroyer avoided the first torpedo, but was hit by the second, set on fire, and completely disabled. Other aircraft went to press on their attacks on the convoy itself. One of them, dropping its torpedo between two merchant vessels hit HMS Manchester as she was turning to regain her station after avoiding two torpedoes fired earlier. She reversed helm once more but to no avail. During the attacks three enemy torpedo bombers were shot down by AA fire from the ships.

HMS Manchester was badly damaged and could only use one engine out of four. At first she could steam only 8 knots. She was ordered to make for Gibraltar with HMS Avon Vale as escort. That evening, further to the westward, they were attacked again by three enemy torpedo planes but their AA gunfire kept the enemy at a distance. Both ships successfully reached Gibraltar on the 26th.

At 1010 B/23 five more bombers tried to attack the convoy crossing this time from north to south. Fighters from HMS Ark Royal forced them to drop their bombs from great height and mostly outside the screen.
At 1645 B/23 five more torpedo planes led by a seaplane came in from the northward. Three Fulmars caught them about 20 miles away. They managed to shoot down two planes and drove the remainder away.

Soon afterwards the fleet arrived off the entrance to the Skerki Channel. There HMS Hermione was transferred to Force X to take the place of HMS Manchester. Six destroyers were assigned to Force H and eight to Force X. At 1713 hours Vice-Admiral Somerville hauled round to the westward. HMS Ark Royal kept her Fulmars up until RAF Beaufighters had arrived from Malta to take over.

The convoy was attacked again around 1900 B/23. Four torpedo planes arrived from the eastward, flying low and and working round from ahead to the starboard side of the convoy. They approached in pairs in line abreast. They kept HMS Sikh (on the starboard bow of the screen) between them and their target until nearly the moment for attack, thereby hampering the AA fire from the other ships. They dropped their torpedoes from long range from a height of 50 feet and nearly hit HMS Hermione, sternmost ship in the starboard column. To avoid the attack each column of the convoy turned 90° outwards and all warships opened barrage fire from all guns that would bear. The barrage however fell short but it caused the Italians to drop their torpedoes early. Also one of the enemy was possibly shot down.

This attack scattered the convoy and it took some time to reform. At 1945 B/23 about seven bombers appeared from ahead at a height of about 14000 feet to attack the convoy from the port side. The convoy altered 40° to port together and the escort opened up a controlled fire with some hesitation as the Italian aircraft looked a lot like Beaufighters. The bombing was extremely accurate. Several bombs fell near HMS Edinburgh which was leading the port column, and a near miss abreast a boiler room disabled HMS Firedrake which had been sweeping ahead of the convoy. She could no longer steam so Rear-Admiral Syfret ordered her back to Gibraltar in tow of HMS Eridge. They had an anxious passage, being shadowed by aircraft continuously during daylight hours, but were not again attacked. On the 25th HMS Firedrake managed to lit one boiler so the tow was slipped. Both destroyers entered Gibraltar harbour on the 27th.

Soon after leaving the Skerki Channel in the evening of the 23th the convoy hauled up to the north-east towards the coast of Sicily. This was to lessen the danger of mines. The Italians did not shadow the convoy after the attack at 1945 hours and missed this alteration of course which they clearly did not expect. Around 2100 hours, as it was getting dark, enemy aircraft were seen searching along its old line of advance. During the evening the convoy sighted flares several times about 20 miles to the south.
Continued passage to the east and enemy attacks, 24 July 1941

Between 0250 and 0315 hours the convoy was however attacked by the Italian MAS boats MAS 532 and MAS 533. The managed to torpedo and damaged the Sydney Star. HMAS Nestor went alongside and took off almost 500 soldiers. Sydney Star was however able to continue her passage as staggler escorted initially by HMAS Nestor. Admiral Syfret however sent back HMS Hermione. At 1000 B/24 eight German dive bombers and two high level bombers attacked. Their bombs fell close the escorting ships. HMS Hermione shot down one dive bomber. The three ships arrived at Malta early in the afternoon.

The main body of the convoy meanwhile continued on its way unhindered after the attacks of the motor torpedo boats except for an attempt by three torpedo planes around 0700 hours. They dropped their torpedoes at a safe distance when fired on by the destroyers in the screen ahead. According to the orders Rear-Admiral Syfret was to leave the convoy now, if there was no threat from Italian surface forces, and go on to Malta with the cruisers and some of the destroyers. They were to land the passengers and stores, complete with fuel and return to Force H as soon as possible. The remaining destroyers were to accompany the transports to Malta. They too were to join Force H as soon as possible. Rear-Admiral Syfret felt easy about the surface danger as all Italian ships were reported in harbour the day before, but he was anxious about the threat to the convoy from the air. He decided to go ahead with the cruiser but leave all destroyers with the convoy so at 0745 B/24, HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa and HMS Manxman left the convoy and pressed ahead at high speed to Malta where they arrived at noon the same day. The transports and the destroyers arrived about four hours later. They had been attacked only once by a torpedo plane since the cruisers separated.
Return passage of the warships of force X to make rendez-vous with Force H.

In the evening HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa, HMS Hermione and HMS Manxman sailed together followed by five destroyers; HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Foxhound, later the same evening. The destroyers overtook the cruisers in the morning of the 25th. The sixth destroyer, HMS Farndale, had to be left at Malta due to defects (condenser problems). All ships made rendez-vous with Force H to the north-west of Galita Island at 0800 B/25.
Movements of Force H after it parted from the convoy.

After parting with the convoy in the evening of the 23rd, Vice-Admiral Somerville had taken force H westward at 18 knots until the afternoon of the 24th going as far west as 03°30’E. He then turned back to meet Admiral Syfret, also sending from HMS Ark Royal six Swordfish aircraft which left her in position 37°42’N, 07°17’E at 1000 B/25. After their junction Forces H and X made the best of way towards Gibraltar. Fighter patrols of HMS Ark Royal shot down a shadowing aircraft soon after the fleet had shaped course to the westward, losing a Fulmar in doing so. However another aircraft had meanwhile reported the fleet.

High level bombers appeared from the east and torpedo bombers from the north at 1100 hours. HMS Ark Royal at that moment had four fighters in the air and sent up six more. They prevented the bombing attack shooting down three aircraft out of eight at a cost of two Fulmars, while the ships watched the enemy jettison their bombs 15 miles away. The torpedo attack came to nothing too for the enemy gave up the attempt and retired while still several miles from the fleet. Two days later, on the 27th, the fleet reached Gibraltar.
The movements of the seven empty ships coming from Malta.

Six of the transports / tankers left Malta for Gibraltar in the morning of the 23rd, escorted by HMS Encounter. The seventh ship, tanker Svenor grounded while leaving harbour and was held up for some hours. At dusk, when a few miles from Pantelleria, the six ships devided into pairs according to their speed. HMS Encounter initially escorted the middle pair but joined the leading ships in the evening of the 24th when past the Galita Bank.

Italian aircraft, both high level bombers and torpedo planes, attacked all these ships on the 24th to the southward of Sardinia. They made their first attempt on the second pair of transports and HMS Encounter. Four torpedo planes attacked at 1230 B/24 and four bombers at 1250 B/24. No ships were hit though the bombs fell close. Next came the turn for the leading pair, which were attacked further westwards by two bombers that came singly at 1330 B/24 and 1400 B/24. The second plane nearly hit HMS Breconshire. Finally when the third pair of ships reached about the same position in the evening they were attacked by torpedo planes and the Hoegh Hood was damaged but she managed to arrive at Gibraltar only a few hours after her consort on the 27th. The last ship, the one that had been delayed at Malta, arrived on the 28th. (8)

22 Jul 1941 At 0500 A/22, south of Cavoli Island, in approximate position 38°54'N, 09°30'E, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted two schooners bearing 180° and 235°. These vessels, thought to be Italian A/S schooners, remained in the vicinity until the end of the forenoon. (7)

23 Jul 1941 At 1121 A/23, east of Serpentara Island, in position 39°09'N, 09°44'E, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted the tops of the mast and funnel of a merchant vessel bearing 255° at a range of 6 miles. This ship was on a southerly course.
At 1317 A/23, the smoke of two vessels was seen on bearing 243°. Range was estimated as being 7 miles.

Outside possibilities: The Italian transports Vittoria Beraldo (547 GRT, built 1918) at 1300 B/22 at 6.5 knots and Egle (1143 GRT, built 1918) at 1500 B/22 at 7.5 knots, were expected to sail both from Civitavecchia for Cagliari. The time does not quite match unless they sailed ahead of schedule. Otherwise, they would have been about 40 miles to the NE of P 32. We do not have the actual time of their departures. (7)

24 Jul 1941 At 0745 A/24, west-south-west of Cavoli Island, in position 39°04'N, 09°27'E, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted smoke of a southbound merchant vessel on bearing 281°. Owing to the rapid change of bearing it was estimated that it would be impossible to close the range sufficiently to attack so no attack was started.

Outside possibilities: The Italian transports Vittoria Beraldo (547 GRT, built 1918) at 1300 B/22 at 6.5 knots and Egle (1143 GRT, built 1918) at 1500 B/22 at 7.5 knots, were expected to sail both from Civitavecchia for Cagliari. The time does not quite match unless they sailed behind of schedule.

At 0800 A/24, HMS P 32 sighted a sailing vessel bearing 360° at a range of 4 miles. Course of this vessel was 180°. Whilst avoiding this vessel, thought to be an A/S schooner, a small outward bound coastal vessel passed close inshore and altered course to pass between Cavoli Island and Cape Carbonara.

At 1000 A/24, smoke was sighted on bearing 228°. This smoke was closed at high speed but it was not possible to estimate course and speed of this northbound merchant vessel which passed ahead out of range.
At 1045 A/24, course was set to patrol on the route of the recent sighted merchant vessels.
At 1535 A/24, in position 38°56'N, 09°21'E, a schooner was sighted bearing 163° at a range of 7 miles. Course of this schooner was 072°. (7)

25 Jul 1941 At 0702 A/25, off Cagliari Bay, south-west of Cavoli Island, in position 38°59'N, 09°21'E, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted a patrol vessel, thought to be a RD-class minesweeper, and a small motor trawler passing two miles away to the eastward. Enemy course was 160°.

At 1000 A/25, the same two vessels were in sight bearing 170° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 330°. HMS P 32 closed to investigate. These ships were too small to be attacked by torpedoes. It was thought both these vessels were minesweeping.
[We have been unable to identify the vessels seen on this day.] (7)
26 Jul 1941 At 0100 A/26, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) sighted a trawler at a range of 3000 yards. HMS P 32 dived to avoid being sighted.
Between 0700 A/26 and around 0950 A/26, the same two vessels as the day before were seen minesweeping.
At 1215 A/26, the two minesweepers were again sighted sweeping the northern end of the suspected swept channel.
At 2120 A/26, HMS P 32 left patrol in accordance with the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1130 B/25. (7)

27 Jul 1941 At 1210 A/27, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) received the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1015 B/27, ordering her to remain west of 11°00'E and to make the submerged passage through the Italian declared minefield during daylight on the 29th. (7)
31 Jul 1941 Around 0710 B/31, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Malta. (7)
12 Aug 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) departed Malta for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off Tripoli, Libya in area 'V', south 33°10'N, and between 13°30'E and 15°00'E. (9)

13 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 2001 B/13, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) of the following; Six small ships in position 35°06'N, 12°30'E, course 187°, speed 8 knots at 1748 Z/13. (10)

14 Aug 1941 In the afternoon, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) a small westbound convoy was sighted, made up of a 1000-ton merchant vessel and two schooners of approximately 200 tons. They were escorted by a Spica or Orsa-class torpedo boat.
They were not attacked in order to not compromise the submarines position so early in the patrol and also as Lt. Abdy had been instructed that eastbound convoy's were much more valuable.

This was the convoy consisting of the steamers Prospero (Italian, 971 GRT, built 1904) and Pertusola (Italian, 1493 GRT, built 1882), the schooners V 56 / Cora (?) and Esperia (385 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Partenope, which sailed from Benghazi at 1740 B/12 and arrived at Tripoli at 1853 B/14. The only incident of note was the sighting of a drifting Italian mine of the P.200 type at 1116 B/13 in position 32°07'N, 17°48'E which was destroyed by Partenope with machine gun fire.

14 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1601 B/14, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) were given the following orders; HMS P 32 was to patrol in position 33°10'N, 13°14'E and HMS P 33 in position 33°20'N, 13°00'E. A 12.5 knots convoy made up of six merchant ships and five destroyers due Tripoli 1300 B/15 and an 8 knots convoy made up of four merchant ships and six destroyers due Tripoli 1630 B/18.

The first convoy referred to was the one made up of the transports Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Francesco Barbaro (Italian, 6343 GRT, built 1940), Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939). This convoy had departed Naples around 1330 B/13-1700 B/13 at 13.5 knots bound for Tripoli. The convoy was escorted by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi (escort leader), Folgore, Strale, Lanzerotto Malocello, Fulmine and the Italian torpedo boat Orsa. At 1835 B/13, in position 167° - Punta Carena - 8.5 miles, a reconnaissance aircraft dropped a white smoke flare and signalled a submarine ahead of Ugolino Vivaldi, followed by two bombs. The destroyer dropped four 100-kg depth charges, three set at 100 metres, and one set at 75 metres, and two 50-kg depth charges set at 50 metres, the torpedo boat Orsa also dropped a number of depth charges. As the Vivaldi forward gun turret opened fire to signal the presence of the submarine to the convoy, a loading accident occurred as the second salvo was fired, wounding thirteen of the gun crew, four of them seriously. At 1905 B/13, Ugolino Vivaldi ordered Orsa to rejoin the convoy and Vivaldi herself turned back to land her wounded in Naples at 2200 B/13, with Folgore now assuming the escort command. There were no enemy submarines in the area, the sighting was 'bogus' as was often the case. The convoy was attacked by enemy bombers at 0105 B/15, 25 miles SW of Lampdedusa. At 1315 B/15-14150 B/15, it arrived at Tripoli without further incident.

The second convoy referred to was the one made up of the transports Caffaro (Italian, 6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926), Maddalena Odero (Italian, 5545 GRT, built 1921), Marin Sanudo (Italian, 5958 GRT, built 1926), Nicolò Odero (Italian, 6003 GRT, built 1925) and the tanker Minatitland (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1941). This was the 40th Seetransport Staffel which had departed Naples around 0030 B/16. It was escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia, Euro, Dardo and the Italian torpedo boats Procione and Pegaso. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Palermo at 1600 B/16 to reinforce the escort. Off Trapani, Procione was detached and entered this port at 1300 B/28. Eight miles south of Lampedusa, Maddalena Odero was torpedoed twice by RAF bombers at 2045 B/17. Pegaso towed her to Lampedusa while Sirtori looked for survivors. Giuseppe Sirtori, Pegaso and the stricken vessel entered this port at 1215 B/18 (or 1020 B/18, sources give different times). At 1300 B/18, Maddalena Odero was hit by a bomb from a RAF aircraft and blew up. Pegaso turned back, arriving at Trapani at 0625 B/19, while Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Lampedusa at 2015 B/18 for Porto Empedocle. The remainder of the convoy reached Tripoli at 1700 B/18-1800 B/18.

This convoy had also been attacked in position 39°35'N, 13°18'E by HrMs O 23 (Lt.Cdr. G.B.M. van Erkel, RNN) at 1004 A/16 (1115 B/16 according to the Italians) from long range but all torpedoes missed. The torpedoes were sighted by Freccia and by a CANT Z.501 seaplane. (10)

15 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1144 B/15, the Cdr. S.1 transmitted the following orders to HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN); My 1601 B/14. At 2100 B/18 unless the second convoy referred to is sighted before then HMS P 32 resume patrol in area V, HMS Unique will proceed to Area T and P 33 proceed direct to position 35°30'N, 14°10'E to arrive at Marsamxett [Malta] before 2030 B/19 or after 0630 B/20.
In a signal timed 2005 B/15, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta ordered HMS P 33 to amend her date of arrival to 20th or 21st. (10)

17 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 2015 B/17, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN), HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) of the following; Second convoy (referred to in the signal timed 1601 B/14) due Tripoli about noon/18. When passed HMS P 33 return to Malta as in my signal timed 1144 B/15. HMS Unique and HMS P 32 proceed to patrol in the vicinity of positions 33°20'N, 13°03'E and 33°17'N, 13°14'E respectively to intercept three large motor ships and six destroyers. Enemy speed speed 17.5 knots. Enemy due Tripoli about noon/20.

The fast convoy referred to was the convoy made up of the troop transports (liners) Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Neptunia (19328 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920). Their escort was made up of the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicoloso Da Recco, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti. (the Italian torpedo boat Orione could not join them due to engine defects). This convoy sailed from Naples at 2250 B/18-0140 B/19.

This convoy was reinforced by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza (sailed from Trapani at 0910 B/19 until Tripoli) off Cape San Vito at 1330 B/19 and at 1450 B/19 from Marettimo by the destroyers Maestrale, Grecale and Scirocco (the destroyers sailed from Palermo at 0730 B/19, the first two escorted the convoy until 0100 B/20 and returned to Palermo, while the third went on to Tripoli).
This convoy reached Tripoli at 1230-1415 B/20. (10)
18 Aug 1941 HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) is lost by an explosion (either internal or a mine) off Tripoli, Libya while attacking an enemy convoy.

The ships HMS P 32 had sighted were part of a convoy made up of the Italian merchants Nicolò Odero (6003 GRT, built 1925), Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924), Marin Sanudo (5958 GRT, built 1926), Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1926) and the Italian tanker Minatitland (7651 GRT, built 1941). Another Italian merchant vessel the Maddalena Odero (5545 GRT, built 1921) had already been torpedoed and damaged by aircraft and was no longer part of this convoy. Escort of this convoy was made up of the Italian destroyers Freccia, Euro and Dardo.

Around 1530 B/18 P 32 was at periscope depth when a convoy of four medium seized merchant ships in line ahead was sighted with an aircraft overhead. No escorts were seen. Started attack on the rear ship that was thought to be a tanker of about 6000 tons. Went to 50 feet and closed at speed.

Around 1540 B/18 when P 32 was returning to periscope depth from 50 feet when at 34 feet an explosion occurred and P 32 took a large angle bow down. She was out of control and hit the bottom at 200 feet where she remained.

The submarine was flooded up from forward up to the control room. Chlorine gas was filling the submarine and it was decided to try to escape from the engine room escape hatch using D.S.E.A. apparatus. The Commanding Officer and two ratings went to the conning tower to try to escape from there. The commanding officer and one of the ratings, the coxwain, successfully did so. The other rating drowned during the attempt. The two survivors were later picked up by an Italian MAS boat and taken to Tripoli.
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
